summary: Stellar second set with rare World‑To‑Give and scorching Jack‑Roe
blurb: The night’s highlight is a blazing second set that rolls from an open‑ended jam into Jack‑A‑Roe, Playing In The Band, Shakedown Street and the rarely‑heard “If I Had The World To Give.” Jerry’s vocals are intimate on the ballad and ferocious on the rockers. The Rhythm Devils provide nonstop propulsion throughout.
review: On November 20 1978 at Cleveland Music Hall the Grateful Dead delivered a show that has become a touchstone for ’78 fans. While the first set is solid, it’s the second set that truly shines – an exploratory jam segues into a thunderous Jack‑A‑Roe, then bursts through two massive Playing In The Band passages before landing on a funk‑charged Shakedown Street and the elusive “If I Had The World To Give.” Jerry Garcia’s voice is described as “intimate” on the ballad and his guitar runs “blasting off with rapid 128th‑note runs” on Peggy‑O, while later reviewers hear a “vulnerable” tone that makes the solos feel freshly emotional. Keith Godchaux’s piano swirls are called “magical,” providing direction for the free‑form improvisations, and the Rhythm Devils (Mickey Hart & Bill Kreutzmann) are praised as “incredible” and “tight” throughout the jam‑drums interludes. Bob Weir was reportedly ill early in the set, missing some of the opening jam, but his slide work resurfaces on Shakedown Street where he adds backing vocals. Despite a few splice gaps and occasional drop‑outs in the tape sources, the performance itself is consistently lauded as one of the most inspired ’78 second sets.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across all recordings: multiple reviewers call the second set a ‘marvel of exploration,’ praise rare “World To Give,” label the jam‑drums flow as ‘incredible,’ and highlight Jerry’s soaring guitar and vocal work. No consistent negative comments about musical performance appear; only technical recording issues are noted, which do not affect show quality.

best_recording.reason: Crisp soundboard capture with clear instrument separation; despite splice gaps it presents the most complete and highest‑fidelity version of the legendary second set.

key_highlights: Rare performance of “If I Had The World To Give” in a powerful ballad setting
Scorching Jack‑A‑Roe → Playing In The Band → Shakedown Street sequence
Jerry Garcia’s intimate vocals and blistering guitar runs, especially on Peggy‑O and the second set jams
Keith Godchaux’s magical piano swirls guiding the free‑form improvisations
song_highlights: If I Had The World To Give

band_performance.Jerry: Intimate vocal on “World To Give” and ferocious, rapid‑run guitar work on Peggy‑O; later described as vulnerable yet soaring, delivering big chords and lemon‑squeezer arpeggios throughout the second set.
band_performance.Bob: Absent for the first ten minutes of the second set due to illness, but his slide‑heavy drones reappear on Shakedown Street where he adds backing vocals; overall impact limited by early absence.
band_performance.Keith: Piano swirls described as perfectly timed and magical during instrumental interludes; wild yet tight playing that often steered the band’s direction in the improvisations.
band_performance.Mickey: Part of the Rhythm Devils praised for “incredible” power throughout the jam, delivering a driving force especially in the opening jam‑drums segment.
band_performance.Billy: Provides tight, energetic drumming that fuels the free‑form vibe; short drum break early in set two called ‘amazingly good’ and supports the seamless flow of the second set.

must_listen_sequences: If I Had The World To Give
